title: Chapter 35: The Bridge
content: Mia's reaction to the Roosevelt was predictable and necessary.

"No," she said, before I'd even finished describing the meeting. "Absolutely not. Sharing Luna's blood and Rose's research with a paramilitary outfit run by an emotionally stunted system host? That's not cooperation, that's surrender."
"He offered supplies—"
"Supplies we can get from the system. What he wants is irreplaceable. Luna's antibodies are a strategic asset, and giving them away for canned beans and ammunition is a terrible trade."

"Mia's right," Sarah said, surprising me. "Colt's being reasonable now because he needs something. Once he has the antibody data, his leverage shifts. We need to negotiate from a position of strength, not gratitude."

They were both right, and I hated it—hated the calculus of allyship, the way every relationship in the apocalypse came with a balance sheet. But that was the world we lived in, and pretending otherwise wouldn't make it gentler.
"So what do we do?" I asked.
"Counter-offer," Mia said. "We share *limited* data—enough to show good faith, not enough to give away the store. In exchange, we get something we can't produce ourselves."
"Like what?"

"Intelligence. His system tracks threats in real-time—tactical mapping, zombie movement patterns, Alpha variant locations. That data is more valuable than supplies. It would let us plan operations, avoid danger zones, maybe even predict where the next evolution will occur."

It was a good idea—Mia's ideas usually were—and the group discussed the specifics with the kind of focused energy that came from having a clear objective. Rose would prepare a sanitized research packet: enough data to demonstrate progress without revealing the full antibody structure. Luna would agree to one blood draw, supervised, with Colt's doctor present. In exchange, we'd get access to the Command System's threat mapping for a thirty-day trial period.
"Fair terms," Rick said, his logistics brain appreciating the symmetry. "Both sides give something they can afford and get something they can't. Classic framework."
"Classic framework assumes both sides honor the deal," Maria said quietly—the first time she'd spoken in a strategy session. "What happens when he decides he wants more?"
"Then we renegotiate," I said. "Or we fight. But we cross that bridge when we come to it."
The group nodded—not unanimous enthusiasm, but collective acceptance. We'd built enough trust to disagree without fracturing, and that was worth more than any system reward.
I went to Luna last.

She was in the garden bay, as always, her hands in the soil, her nose inches from a row of tomato seedlings that had sprouted overnight. The hydroponic lights cast her face in green and gold, and for a moment—just a moment—she looked like a painting, a Renaissance Madonna tending her garden at the end of the world.
"You're staring," she said without looking up.
"I'm appreciating."
"Those are the same thing."
"Different connotations. Appreciating implies gratitude."
She looked up then, and the smile she gave me was the full, real thing—the Luna smile, rare and radiant, that made the titanium walls feel like they could breathe.
"What do you need?" she asked.
"Your blood. Again. One more draw, this time with Colt's doctor present. It's part of the deal we're negotiating."
"Do I have to?"
"No. It's your blood. Your choice. Always."

She considered this with the careful attention she brought to all decisions. Her hand went to the inside of her elbow—the place where Rose drew the samples, the spot that still made her flinch despite weeks of practice.
"Will it help people?" she asked.
"Possibly. Colt's doctor might see something Rose hasn't. Two minds are better than one."
"Will it hurt me?"
"A little. Your regeneration handles it within hours."
"Will it give Colt power over us?"
I hesitated. The honest answer was *maybe*, and Luna deserved honesty.
"Maybe," I said. "It's a risk. But it's a risk I think is worth taking, and I won't ask you to take it unless you agree."
She was quiet for a long moment. Then she wiped her hands on her shirt and extended her arm, palm up, the inside of her elbow exposed—a gesture of trust so complete it made my chest ache.

"You have never lied to me," she said. "You have never forced me. You have always asked. So I will give this, because you ask, and because I trust your judgment, and because I was dead and you gave me life and a little blood is a small price for everything."
I took her hand—not the extended arm, but the hand, the gesture that had started everything.
"Thank you," I said.
"Don't thank me. Love me. That's the price."
"Paid in full," I said, and kissed her palm, and felt the warmth of her skin against my lips—the warmth that shouldn't exist in a dead woman but did, because the system was more than a leash.
It was a bridge. Between the dead and the living. Between the alone and the together. Between the end of the world and whatever came after.
And we were building it, one choice at a time, one drop of blood at a time, one *stay* at a time.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: LUNA — 78%**
Climbing toward completion. Not because the system demanded it—because Luna chose it.
Because love wasn't a quest reward. It was the quest itself.
The next morning, I radioed Colt.
"Deal," I said. "Limited data sharing, one supervised blood draw, thirty days of tactical mapping. Your terms, plus one addition."
"What addition?"
"When we find out who designed these systems—and we will—you share that information with me. Full disclosure. No holding back."
A pause. The sound of Colt thinking—the tactical mind running scenarios, weighing outcomes.
"Agreed," he said. "When we find the architect, we face them together."
"Together," I said, and meant it, even though the voice that sounded like Mia was still whispering warnings.
Some bridges, you had to cross to find out if they'd hold.
This one, at least, we'd cross with our eyes open.
